architecture_implications: Clip per-example or per-person contributions before adding noise so a prolific correspondent cannot dominate updates to a personalized model.
Keep the privacy accountant, training configuration, and release decision in the governance plane so every model refresh has a traceable cumulative budget.
Treat privacy as an end-to-end, auditable budget attached to a defined privacy unit rather than as a generic claim about encrypted storage.
core_finding: The method trained useful deep models at a stated privacy budget; at epsilon 8 and delta 1e-5, the reported models reached about 97% accuracy on MNIST and 73% on CIFAR-10, with a much larger utility cost on the harder task.
limitations: The experiments use public image benchmarks rather than conversational or personalized language data. The guarantee depends on the chosen privacy unit, clipping norm, sampling assumptions, and budget; it does not by itself enforce contextual consent, deletion, or output access control.
method: The paper introduces differentially private stochastic gradient descent with per-example gradient clipping, Gaussian noise, and a moments accountant for tighter cumulative privacy accounting, then evaluates the method on image classification.
population_or_data: Public MNIST and CIFAR-10 image benchmarks used to train and evaluate non-convex neural networks under several privacy budgets.

core_finding: Privacy concern weakly predicted Facebook membership, and concerned users still revealed substantial information; participants also held mistaken beliefs about community size, composition, and profile visibility.
limitations: The study covers early Facebook at one United States academic institution, before major changes in platform population and privacy controls. The design documents attitude-behavior gaps but does not identify a single causal mechanism.
method: A representative institutional survey was compared with information retrieved from Facebook profiles, including analysis of membership, disclosure, privacy concern, and reactions to privacy-related exposure.
population_or_data: Members and nonmembers of Facebook at one United States academic institution; sample size is not stated in the inspected publisher abstract.

architecture_implications: A digital brain may surface owner-described changes in agency over time, but it must not convert this clinical group association into an individual mental-health inference.
Track agency and coherence independently and avoid using polished coherence as a proxy for owner health, adaptation, or narrative fidelity.
core_finding: Agency increased and its increases preceded associated mental-health improvement, whereas coherence did not reliably increase, showing that the two narrative dimensions followed different trajectories.
limitations: The treatment-seeking sample was small and lacked a randomized control condition, narratives were repeatedly prompted in a psychotherapy context, and lagged association does not rule out treatment or other time-varying causes.
method: Adults wrote personal narratives before therapy and after each session across 12 assessment points while completing mental-health measures; nearly 600 narratives were coded and analyzed with multilevel and lagged growth models.

limitations: The study has only ten participants, short tasks, and a keyword news model, so effect estimates are fragile. Novelty and redundancy problems may have motivated edits, confounding preference correction with interface-level frustration.
method: A within-participant study compares a hidden profile with an editable keyword profile in YourNews across two timed news-finding sessions.

architecture_implications: Do not treat refusals or low leakage under ordinary prompts as evidence of privacy; red-team the boundary between aligned chat behavior and base-model behavior.
Repeat extraction audits after every model or alignment update because the demonstrated production attack was endpoint-specific.
Track unique leaked sequences and sensitive-content severity in addition to the fraction of generated tokens that match training data.
core_finding: Alignment made standard extraction probes appear much less successful, but targeted attacks recovered more than ten thousand training examples from the tested ChatGPT deployment at roughly 200 dollars in query cost. The attack raised observed training-data emission dramatically over prior probing.
limitations: The production-model attack was specific to model versions tested in 2023 and the proprietary training corpus was unavailable, so web-corpus matching yields a lower bound and cannot prove every match was in training. Attack effectiveness, endpoints, and safeguards can change over time.
method: The authors evaluated memorization across open, semi-closed, and production models, built auxiliary web-scale corpora for match verification, and developed two attacks that make aligned chat models behave more like base models and emit training data.

architecture_implications: Represent self-knowledge as a repertoire of relationship-scoped schemas rather than one global persona, with explicit links between a person or relationship cue and the self-state it may activate.
Treat relationship-conditioned retrieval as probabilistic: preserve a stable default while recording which contextual cues supported each relational-self hypothesis and how certain that linkage is.
core_finding: Knowledge about significant others is linked in memory with relationship-specific self-knowledge; cues that activate a significant-other representation can evoke the corresponding relational self, while chronic accessibility helps produce recurring patterns.
limitations: This is a theory-building review rather than a new representative study. The experiments it integrates use constrained transference paradigms, so the account does not establish how completely relational selves explain natural longitudinal messaging or culturally diverse relationships.
method: Theoretical integration of experimental social-cognitive research on transference, significant-other representations, self-evaluation, motivation, and self-regulation.
population_or_data: Published experimental evidence on significant-other representations and transference; the article does not report a new participant sample.

architecture_implications: Distinguish within-session consistency from calibrated absolute preference and retain the elicitation history and any numerical or social anchors attached to a valuation.
Use anchor-robust comparisons and repeated owner elicitation before treating a price, intensity, or priority estimate as a stable personal parameter.
core_finding: Arbitrary anchors strongly shifted initial valuations, while subsequent valuations remained coherently ordered; experience and some market mechanisms did not reliably remove the anchor's influence.
limitations: The experiments concern laboratory valuations of consumer goods and short hedonic experiences. Artificial anchors may not approximate autobiographical goals, and the results do not imply that every coherent preference pattern lacks a stable component.
method: Six experiments manipulated arbitrary anchors for familiar products and simple hedonic experiences, then measured subsequent valuations across quality or quantity changes and market-like conditions.
population_or_data: Experimental participants valued familiar products and hedonic experiences after arbitrary anchoring manipulations; the primary abstract does not report sample sizes or demographics.

core_finding: Participants reported improved usefulness, transparency, and controllability after controls were introduced, while trust and privacy concerns remained.
limitations: Only seven participants were studied, with no control group or counterbalancing, so phase order and novelty can explain part of the improvement. The four-week professional-document setting does not demonstrate durable behavior, objective accuracy gains, or safe correction at scale.
method: A two-phase field study first exposes a personalized literature portal without controls and then adds user-model inspection, editing, explanations, recommendation overrides, and on-off controls.

architecture_implications: Record observability and task assumptions with every inferred motive, because inverse planning is only meaningful relative to what options, costs, and information the modeled person plausibly had.

Use a generative latent-state model that explicitly separates what an agent can perceive, believe, and desire, then maintain a posterior over alternatives rather than writing the highest-scoring motive as fact.
core_finding: The full model closely tracked quantitative human judgments across two experiments and outperformed versions missing mental-state components and models based only on visible motion features.
limitations: The experiments use stylized spatial agents and assume approximately rational action under a known task model; Experiment 1 is small. Agreement with human judgments does not verify the agent's actual hidden state, and everyday behavior may involve habit, coercion, conflicting goals, or unknown options.
method: A Bayesian theory-of-mind model inverted sequential planning in partially observable spatial environments and was compared with human ratings, lesioned models, and nonmentalistic motion-feature models in two experiments.
population_or_data: Experiment 1 compared model and mental-state judgments from 16 participants across spatial scenarios; Experiment 2 compared percept inferences from 176 participants across its scenario set.

Give recent observed behavior explicit weight while preserving stable persona evidence and their provenance.
Support branch correction after a bad prediction so one hallucinated action does not become false history.

limitations: Personas and chains come largely from English-language Western literature and biographies rather than longitudinal logs of consenting users; source extraction is partly synthetic, generation scoring relies on an LLM judge, and human evaluation covers only a subset.

core_finding: Speakers orient most strongly to direct addressees, but auditors, overhearers, and absent reference groups can also shape style; some shifts respond to a changed audience while others proactively redefine the situation.
limitations: The article is a foundational theoretical account centered on sociolinguistic and mass-communication cases before contemporary social platforms. It does not test individualized chat generation, algorithmic audience selection, or private multi-party messaging.
method: A sociolinguistic theory paper synthesizing evidence on style shifting, accommodation, code choice, and mass communication.

limitations: The data come from United States Facebook users with friends-only posts in 2012. The exposure event indicates viewport display rather than attention, the study does not identify audience composition, and platform ranking has changed.
method: Two surveys elicited general and post-specific audience estimates and explanations, then compared them with server-side exposure logs; a larger log analysis tested possible visible audience heuristics.
population_or_data: 542 respondents to a general-audience survey, 589 to a specific-post survey, and one month of friends-only post exposure logs from about 222,000 United States Facebook users.

architecture_implications: Store event specificity, owner-authored meaning, content category, and recalled affect as separate fields rather than collapsing a self-defining memory into one embedding or importance score.

limitations: The sample was a single undergraduate convenience group, the analyses were correlational and based on self-report, and coding compresses complex narratives; the study did not test longitudinal stability or whether elicited meanings were causally beneficial.
method: Researchers developed coding protocols for specificity, autobiographical meaning, and affect, combined them with an existing content scheme, double-coded 1,040 memories, and related participant-level dimensions to an adjustment inventory.

architecture_implications: Maintain a data-to-artifact lineage map from source records through shards, adapters, indexes, checkpoints, and releases so a deletion request has an executable scope.
Partition personalized learning by person or source where feasible, then aggregate at inference time to limit the retraining blast radius.

limitations: The evaluation centers on supervised classifiers and simulated requests, not generative models or person-level conversational memories. SISA stores and serves multiple models, and its speedup depends on task complexity, shard accuracy, request distribution, and checkpoint storage.
method: SISA training shards the data, isolates shard models, slices training into checkpoints, and aggregates predictions; experiments compare unlearning time and accuracy across datasets and request distributions.

architecture_implications: Ask only when an answer is expected to change an owner-facing decision, not merely because a belief is uncertain.

limitations: The one-step objective can miss combinations of questions whose joint value is high even when each question looks weak alone. The method assumes an explicit probabilistic preference model, rateable items, and query-cost estimates validated only on offline movie data.
method: The paper derives myopic expected-value-of-information queries for probabilistic collaborative filters and uses offline query prototypes to reduce online computation.

architecture_implications: Keep retrieved evidence outside model weights when possible, with authorization checked before each retrieval and with generated output scanned for unsupported verbatim disclosure.
Make adversarial training-data extraction a release gate for any model trained or fine-tuned on personal communications.
Place canary, verbatim-match, and PII extraction tests in the release gate for any model trained or fine-tuned on personal communications.
Test both unprompted sampling and context-seeded attacks, and verify candidate leaks against an access-controlled provenance index.
Treat model access as access to a potentially lossy copy of training data; logging, query controls, and incident response must cover extraction attempts.
Treat verbatim private-text emission as a fidelity failure even when it makes an answer appear more personally accurate.
core_finding: The audit verified 604 memorized training examples, including 78 examples containing personally identifiable information. Larger models exposed more memorized text, and extraction did not require obvious global overfitting.
limitations: The target was the GPT-2 family trained on public WebText, and the untargeted manual audit establishes a lower bound rather than a complete leakage rate. It does not estimate risks for private chat archives, retrieval systems, or later model architectures.
method: The study generated three sets of 200,000 GPT-2 samples, ranked candidates using six memorization scores, manually inspected 1,800 candidates, and searched the model's documented training corpus to verify memorization.
population_or_data: GPT-2 models trained on WebText, 600,000 generated samples, 1,800 high-ranked candidates, and the corresponding public web training corpus used for verification.

architecture_implications: Calibrate automated judges against blinded human ratings on the intended language and cultural domain before using them for model selection.
Preserve turn-level rationales and dimension scores so a strong style score cannot hide knowledge or behavioral failures.

Track duplicate groups and source provenance so deletion and privacy accounting operate on all copies rather than one row.
core_finding: Regeneration increased superlinearly with duplication: a sequence appearing ten times was generated roughly 1,000 times as often as a unique sequence. limitations: The evaluated attacks focus on exact sequences in web corpora and a limited set of model sizes and membership scores. Deduplication is not a formal privacy guarantee, may remove legitimately repeated evidence, and does not address unique PII or inference-time disclosure.
method: The authors measure exact-sequence duplication in OpenWebText and C4, train 117M-, 345M-, and 1.5B-parameter language models on duplicated and deduplicated corpora, and compare generation and membership-inference attacks.
population_or_data: A 39 GB OpenWebText corpus, a 750 GB C4 corpus, exact 400-character training sequences, several model sizes, and one million generated samples for the deduplication comparison.

architecture_implications: Prefer flexible measurement models and inspect cross-loadings rather than forcing every linguistic or questionnaire feature to represent only one trait.
Record the exact populations and partial-invariance exceptions under which cross-group score comparisons are defensible.
core_finding: The best-fitting model allowed many cross-loadings and generally supported reasonable or partial invariance across language, country, and gender, while some item parameters and criterion correlations still differed across groups.
limitations: The evidence is limited to college students in three countries, the English version needed further replication, some language comparisons required partial rather than full invariance, and tested external criteria were concentrated in mental health and substance use.
method: Exploratory structural equation modeling and ESEM-within-CFA tested configural through increasingly strict invariance, internal consistency, and associations with mental-health and substance-use criteria.
population_or_data: 2,158 college students from the United States (1,117), Argentina (353), and Spain (688) completing online questionnaires.

architecture_implications: Do not turn messaging volume or duration into a global personality claim; demographic, life-stage, and opportunity variables can dominate the same trace.
Keep content inaccessible by default and distinguish low-risk usage metadata from any separately consented semantic analysis.
core_finding: WhatsApp duration had small associations with extraversion, conscientiousness, and neuroticism, no clear association with openness or agreeableness, and substantially stronger variation by age and gender.
limitations: The nonrepresentative, mostly German sample used a two-item-per-trait BFI-10, recorded only visual-use duration rather than message counts or content, and supported correlations rather than causal or individual-level diagnostic inference.
method: A consented Android app logged visual app sessions for at least four weeks; daily WhatsApp duration was correlated with age, gender, education, and a 10-item Big Five measure.
population_or_data: 2,418 media-recruited users, probably mostly in Germany, with a mean age of 24.64 years; 2,281 showed WhatsApp activity.

architecture_implications: Require at least metric and scalar invariance evidence before comparing trait magnitudes across language or cultural groups.
Store item- and feature-level bias diagnostics; matching a broad factor shape is insufficient to justify a common scoring scale.
core_finding: All scales supported a broadly similar configuration, but metric and scalar invariance failed and several adjectives showed item-level bias, undermining direct comparison of observed scores across cultures.
limitations: The study tested one adjective instrument in university samples, with culture, country, and language intertwined; failure for these Mini-Markers does not imply that every Big Five instrument or behavioral model is noninvariant.
method: Mean and Covariance Structures and confirmatory factor analyses tested configural, metric, and scalar equivalence, followed by item-level bias analysis.
population_or_data: Two U.S. undergraduate samples of 727 and 712 respondents, plus 991 Greek and 433 Chinese university students completing the 40-item Big Five Mini-Markers.

architecture_implications: Use language assessment as a revisable complementary signal, not a replacement for explicit self-report or owner correction.
Validate any language-derived trait against multiple owner-approved criteria and report reliability over time rather than presenting an unqualified profile.
core_finding: Language-based scores converged with self- and informant reports, discriminated among traits, added some validity beyond informants, reproduced external-correlation patterns, and were stable across six-month windows.
limitations: The labels and validation came from Facebook volunteers in the myPersonality ecosystem, stability covered six months rather than life-course change, and performance does not establish causal mechanisms, private motives, or transport to other platforms and relationships.
method: Open-vocabulary models were trained from Facebook language and questionnaire scores, then evaluated in a separate sample against self-reports, informant reports, trait discrimination, external correlates, and six-month test-retest stability.
population_or_data: 66,732 Facebook users for model development and a separate 4,824-user evaluation sample with language, Big Five self-reports, and subsets of informant or external-criterion data.

architecture_implications: Estimate reliability from sufficient text across occasions and validate language features against independent owner and behavioral criteria before use.
Treat lexical style as a noisy auxiliary measurement channel, not as a deterministic personality label.
core_finding: Several language-use dimensions were internally consistent and a four-factor linguistic structure replicated across writing samples, but associations with personality and external measures were generally modest.
limitations: The studies mixed small and heterogeneous, often prompted samples; category counts omit semantics and interaction context, and modest associations do not establish a unique psychological cause for a word pattern.
method: Multiple studies assessed internal consistency, factor structure, replication, and convergent associations of computerized word categories across diaries, prompted writing, abstracts, essays, self-reports, behavioral measures, and health markers.
population_or_data: Samples included 15 inpatients' diaries, 35 students' writing, 40 psychologists' abstracts, essays from 838 students with a 381-student replication, a 79-student multimethod comparison, and self-report or health data from more than 1,200 students.

architecture_implications: Do not treat a statistical correction as universal; revalidate the construct after correction in each target population.
Estimate response styles as nuisance parameters when explicit questionnaires supervise or correct a digital-brain profile.
core_finding: Acquiescence correction improved factor-pattern congruence in every country and reached acceptable correspondence throughout individualistic countries, but not consistently in non-individualistic countries.
limitations: The abstract does not establish full measurement invariance or criterion validity, and country-level individualism may stand in for multiple social and survey-process differences; results apply to questionnaire scoring, not directly to language traces.
method: Big Five scores from representative adult samples in 18 countries were corrected for individual acquiescence and compared with an idealized factor-loading pattern across national contexts.
population_or_data: Large adult-population samples designed to be representative within each of 18 countries, spanning individualistic and non-individualistic settings.

architecture_implications: Block personality-model reuse across platforms, relationships, languages, or aggregation levels until target-domain drift and validity checks pass.
Monitor score distributions, temporal stability, and known-null relationships, and abstain when a new local meme or vocabulary shift drives an estimate.
core_finding: Naive cross-platform and cross-level application produced implausible extreme scores and spurious associations; target-side adaptation reduced outliers, improved temporal stability, and generally improved expected external correlations.
limitations: Most counties lacked direct personality labels, so several checks relied on expected distributions and correlates; ecological county results cannot validate individual inference, and the U.S. Facebook-to-Twitter setting is narrow.
method: Target Side Domain Adaptation adjusted county outlier frequencies and Facebook-Twitter frequency shifts, then evaluated predicted Big Five aggregates using distributional plausibility, year-to-year stability, limited state-level ground truth, and external county outcomes.
population_or_data: Facebook models trained from 65,896 users with status text and personality questionnaires; more than 150 million geolocated tweets from 2012-2014 covering 2,197 to 2,651 U.S. counties per year.

architecture_implications: Estimate nuisance effects such as age, language variety, and relationship context before assigning a language pattern to personality.
Version language models by platform and population, and attach source-domain limits to every generated trait estimate.
core_finding: Open-vocabulary features distinguished personality, age, and gender and carried more out-of-sample predictive information than fixed LIWC categories, but personality associations were trait-dependent and language patterns were also strongly demographic and contextual.
limitations: The self-selected Facebook sample excluded low-volume writers and adults over 65, only age and gender were modeled as key covariates, correlations were not causal, and the authors explicitly cautioned that language findings may not generalize across domains.
method: Differential language analysis extracted words, phrases, and topics from Facebook status updates; regressions adjusted associations for age and gender, and out-of-sample models compared open-vocabulary features with LIWC categories.
population_or_data: About 75,000 English-speaking Facebook volunteers with at least 1,000 status words, contributing more than 15.4 million messages and 700 million word, phrase, and topic instances plus personality questionnaires.

architecture_implications: Expose separate person-effect and context-effect contributions, with uncertainty, when explaining a digital-brain inference.
Store situation descriptors and interaction context alongside behavioral evidence so a transient expression is not automatically attributed to personality.
core_finding: Personality traits and situation characteristics each independently predicted momentary behavior and emotion, supporting an additive account rather than a trait-only explanation of observed conduct.
limitations: The study used an intensive but bounded self-report design and did not test language-model transfer; additive average effects do not establish that a specific message reveals a stable trait or its cause.
method: Experience sampling combined trait assessment with repeated real-time measures of situation characteristics, behavior, and emotion, then modeled person and situation effects together.
population_or_data: 210 participants providing intensive real-time reports of situations, behavior, and emotion.

architecture_implications: Do not assume one scoring model or threshold is invariant across developmental stages.
Treat age, comprehension, and response style as measurement conditions, not personality, when calibrating an owner questionnaire or language-derived target.
core_finding: Younger respondents showed much greater acquiescent response variation, and Big Five reports generally became more coherent within domains and more differentiated between domains with age, with distinct trajectories by trait.
limitations: The evidence is cross-sectional and Internet self-selected, uses one questionnaire and administration mode, and cannot separate every age effect from cohort or selection; it concerns self-report psychometrics rather than message-based inference.
method: Year-by-year psychometric analyses of a large, diverse Internet sample examined response-style variability, within-domain coherence, between-domain differentiation, factor structure, and item comprehension from ages 10 to 20.
population_or_data: A large self-selected Internet sample of respondents aged 10 through 20 completing Big Five self-reports.

architecture_implications: Calibrate and report inference quality separately for every trait or facet; a successful model for sociability is not evidence for a general personality decoder.
Prefer minimally sufficient, consented metadata and retain an explicit no-inference state when a criterion is not predictably observed.
core_finding: Behavior predicted 57% of tested domains and facets with moderate correlations, led by communication and social metadata, but predictability varied sharply by construct and agreeableness was not predicted at all.
limitations: The sample was young, educated, German, and Android-only, observation lasted 30 days, behavior was compared with self-reported trait scores, age and gender were not model predictors, and out-of-sample performance within this sample does not prove cultural or device transfer.
method: Elastic-net and random-forest models used 1,821 predictors from six behavior classes, with nested and repeated cross-validation, corrected significance tests, and permutation-based feature interpretation.
population_or_data: 624 predominantly young, highly educated volunteers using Android phones in Germany, observed for 30 days with 25,347,089 logging events and a 300-item German Big Five measure.

architecture_implications: Keep topic choice, lexical style, demographics, and platform selection as possible alternative explanations instead of interpreting a correlation as a trait mechanism.
Track evidence volume and per-feature reliability because sparse words and short histories cannot support the same inference as long longitudinal text.
core_finding: Large writing samples produced reliable word-use estimates and replicated a meaningful subset of earlier language-personality associations, while also revealing many specific correlates; openness had substantially stronger language associations than the other traits.
limitations: The self-selected Blogger sample was 75% female, simple word counts ignored semantics and context, response and platform selection limit representativeness, and the exploratory correlations do not establish causal or out-of-platform validity.
method: Spearman correlations linked LIWC categories and individual word frequencies to IPIP Big Five and facet scores; split-half analyses assessed lexical reliability and prior category associations were directly compared for replication.
population_or_data: 694 Blogger authors averaging 115,423 words over 23.9 months; 576 had Big Five scores and 431 also had facet scores.

architecture_implications: Compare inference channels under equivalent information and measurement conditions before declaring one more accurate.
Maintain separate trace-based, self-reported, and observer-reputation views instead of collapsing agreement with one criterion into a claim of true identity.
core_finding: At the average available Like count, computer scores agreed more with self-ratings than a single friend's brief rating and showed higher external validity on most tested outcomes; computer and human judgments also retained largely distinct information after accounting for self-ratings.
limitations: The computer had many Likes while friends answered a 10-item instrument, self-ratings anchored the main accuracy criterion, all data came from the Facebook myPersonality setting, and Big Five accuracy does not imply a complete or privileged model of a person.
method: LASSO models with 10-fold cross-validation predicted Big Five self-ratings from Likes and were compared with friend ratings and 13 external criteria.
population_or_data: 86,220 volunteers completed a 100-item personality measure; Like-based estimates were available for 70,520, one-friend ratings for 17,622, and two-friend ratings for 14,410 participants.

alternate_ids.value: Quantifying Memorization Across Neural Language Models (2023)

architecture_implications: Deduplicate repeated personal messages before any training use and test long-context prompts that may unlock otherwise hidden sequences.
Maintain model-family-specific privacy baselines because parameter count alone does not predict memorization across architectures and datasets.
Stratify leakage tests by message duplication, context length, and model scale rather than reporting one aggregate extraction rate.
core_finding: Within model families, extractable memorization increased approximately log-linearly with model size, example duplication, and available context. Larger GPT-Neo models memorized two to five times more than smaller variants, while cross-family differences complicated simple scaling predictions.
limitations: The operational definition requires exact greedy continuation after a true training prefix and therefore measures discoverable worst-case memorization, not all semantic leakage or an attacker's practical knowledge. Estimates use samples rather than exhaustive corpora and mostly public base-model families.
method: Using models with known training corpora, the study prompted models with training prefixes and checked exact 50-token continuations across uniform and duplication-normalized samples, model sizes, context lengths, and model families.
population_or_data: GPT-Neo models from 125 million to 6 billion parameters trained on the Pile, with additional GPT-2 and OPT family comparisons and hundreds of thousands of sampled training sequences.

architecture_implications: Choose owner questions by expected information gain, answerability, interaction cost, and coverage rather than uncertainty alone.
Inject limited exploration and audit which domains are never queried so elicitation does not reinforce an already narrow profile.
core_finding: Popularity, popularity-times-entropy, and item-item personalized strategies offered useful accuracy-effort tradeoffs, whereas pure entropy performed poorly in a pilot; the paper also warns that popularity can concentrate exposure and personalization can narrow coverage.
limitations: The study concerns movie ratings during signup, not corrections or drift in a mature longitudinal profile. Known-item ratings are easier to elicit than nuanced personal beliefs, and the short interaction does not measure durable satisfaction or burden.
method: The study compares six elicitation strategies using offline simulations on MovieLens and a live MovieLens deployment with more than 300 new users.
population_or_data: About 7.5 million historical MovieLens ratings for simulation and more than 300 newly registering users in the online study.

architecture_implications: Evaluate elicitation with both downstream model improvement and observed interaction burden; self-reported effort alone can miss friction.
Offer owner-selected and system-selected correction paths, because an efficient algorithmic query policy does not replace autonomy over what to disclose.
core_finding: A system-controlled information-gain strategy gave the best overall accuracy while popularity performed worst; all conditions required manageable effort, and measured effort differences were not always reflected in users' subjective reports.
limitations: The study covers 20 days of movie onboarding and does not observe established-profile drift or repeated correction cycles. Its candidate questions depend on an existing community rating matrix and may not transfer to sparse, private, or unique personal facts.
method: The paper combines offline simulations with a 20-day live MovieLens experiment comparing system-selected information-gain variants, popularity, and user-selected ratings.
population_or_data: About 11,000 MovieLens users, 9,000 movies, and 3 million ratings; 468 people registered for the live experiment, 381 completed it, and 305 answered the exit survey.

architecture_implications: Offer owner-generated 'I am' statements as retrieval pivots, record when each self-image reportedly emerged, and link each image to supporting and contradicting memories rather than converting it directly into a timeless trait.
Represent concrete roles and abstract qualities separately because their temporal anchoring and stability differed, and allow an owner to revise formation dates without deleting earlier estimates.
core_finding: Memories clustered around the reported emergence of the self-image that cued them in both studies, with tighter clustering for concrete identities; formation dates and recognition of identity statements also showed some four-year stability.
limitations: The samples were modest and self-selected, all dates were retrospective, memory accuracy was not verified, and only 13 participants completed the four-year follow-up. Negative self-images were too rare for formal analysis, and concrete identities were often marked by normative events, limiting generalization.
method: Two studies asked participants to generate identity-defining 'I am' statements, select important statements, retrieve multiple memories for each, and retrospectively date both memories and identity formation; a subset completed a four-year follow-up.
population_or_data: Study 1 included 16 community adults aged 47-66 and collected 480 memories; Study 2 included 59 adults aged 39-76 and collected 1,818 memories; 13 Study 1 participants completed the follow-up.

architecture_implications: Evaluate narrative generation against age- and task-appropriate dimensions, and distinguish single-event coherence from coherence of an entire life story.
Preserve the original narrative and annotate context, chronology, and theme separately so a weakness in one dimension cannot erase useful evidence in another.
core_finding: Context, chronology, and theme behaved as distinguishable dimensions with different developmental requirements and trajectories, supporting a multidimensional coding scheme rather than one global coherence construct.
limitations: The pooled application was preliminary and heterogeneous: prompts and event types were partly confounded with age, most participants were European-descended and middle-class, preschool coherence was often correlated with narrative length, and the scheme was not tested for cross-cultural measurement invariance, complete life stories, or digital profiles.
method: The authors derived the Narrative Coherence Coding Scheme from developmental evidence and applied it to 498 personal narratives pooled from six laboratories and participants ranging from age 3 to adulthood.
population_or_data: Four hundred ninety-eight personal-event narratives from six laboratories, spanning preschool-aged children through adults.

architecture_implications: Keep enduring affinity and immediate transition context as separate estimators that can contribute independently to a prediction.
Store transition-derived state with a short validity window rather than promoting the latest action directly into the durable owner profile.
core_finding: The joint personalized-transition model outperformed matrix factorization, a shared factorized Markov chain, and a conventional Markov chain on both sparse and dense next-basket tests.
limitations: The model uses only first-order relative basket order and ignores absolute elapsed time and broader context. Results come from one retail corpus, and purchases are behavior traces rather than confirmed preferences or identity claims.
method: Factorized Personalized Markov Chains factorize a user-specific item-transition tensor and optimize personalized ranking, combining matrix-factorization preference with first-order Markov transitions.
population_or_data: An anonymized drug-store purchase corpus with 71,602 users, 7,180 items, and 233,476 baskets, plus a denser 10,000-user, 1,002-item subset; each user's final basket is held out.

architecture_implications: Represent decision behavior as a context-conditioned repertoire of policies with uncertain expected performance, not as one global decision style.
Update policy weights from outcome feedback while retaining alternatives and provenance, so adaptation in one environment does not overwrite policies useful elsewhere.
core_finding: Participants improved with feedback and increasingly behaved in line with the best-performing strategy available in the studied environments; the strategy-selection learning model predicted their inferences better than the compared alternatives.
limitations: The experiments use constrained probabilistic inference tasks and assume a small researcher-specified strategy repertoire. Reinforcement of task strategies may not transfer to moral, relational, or high-stakes personal decisions, and the primary abstract omits the sample composition needed to judge external validity.
method: Four laboratory studies gave participants repeated probabilistic inference problems with feedback and compared the proposed strategy-selection learning model with extensions and an exemplar-based alternative.
population_or_data: Participants in four computerized inference experiments made repeated choices under feedback; sample sizes and demographic composition are not reported in the primary abstract.

architecture_implications: Evaluate detectors on unseen generators, codecs, crops, and adversarial post-processing before relying on them for moderation or impersonation warnings.
Provide a visible synthetic-media disclosure and a rapid takedown or contest path for the represented person, independent of detector confidence.
Run likeness outputs through manipulation detection and provenance checks, but require positive authorization and generation records because a detector score cannot establish consent or identity.
core_finding: Human accuracy fell from 68.69% on raw videos to 58.73% on low-quality videos. A face-cropped XceptionNet reached 99.26%, 95.73%, and 81.00% on raw, high-quality, and low-quality benchmark data, but performance varied by manipulation and compression.
limitations: The benchmark covers four 2018-era manipulation methods and controlled source videos; in-domain accuracy does not demonstrate generalization to later generators or adversarial conditions. Detection addresses artifacts, not authorization, intent, provenance, or whether the depicted person consented.
method: The authors create a benchmark from four manipulation pipelines, apply raw, high-quality, and low-quality compression, compare hand-crafted and neural detectors, run a 204-participant human study, and provide a hidden test benchmark.
population_or_data: More than 1.8 million manipulated images from 4,000 fake videos generated from 1,000 source videos using DeepFakes, Face2Face, FaceSwap, and NeuralTextures, plus 204 mostly computer-science student observers.

architecture_implications: Assume watermark removal and forgery attempts: rotate keys or detectors, monitor false positives, and never use watermark absence as proof that audio is authentic.

For synthetic voice generated under the system's control, embed a watermark at generation time where compatible and bind the payload to a model or release identifier; passive deepfake detection alone is not a provenance guarantee.
Preserve an independently signed generation log so a watermark can be checked against who authorized the voice, source consent, purpose, and time.
core_finding: AudioSeal achieved an average AUC of 0.97 across tested edits versus 0.84 for WavMark, localized watermarked segments, and detected in one pass up to roughly two orders of magnitude faster. White-box access enabled effective removal attacks, so secrecy of detector weights remained important.
limitations: The watermark only covers generators integrated with the scheme and does not establish consent to use a person's voice. Robustness is empirical over selected edits and attacks; white-box access substantially weakened detection, and deployment requires confidential detector material and false-positive management.
method: AudioSeal jointly trains a waveform watermark generator and single-pass detector with localization and auditory-masking losses, then tests perceptual quality, edits, cross-domain generalization, localization, attribution, speed, and adversarial removal.
population_or_data: Training on a 4,500-hour VoxPopuli subset; evaluation on LibriSpeech, 10,000-sample VoxPopuli edit sets, ASVspoof, FakeAVCeleb, translated Expresso speech, MusicGen, AudioGen, human MUSHRA ratings, and adversarially attacked clips.

architecture_implications: Do not force identity into a model's available labels; preserve a person's own wording and distinguish self-description from system inference.
Expose provenance, uncertainty, and a correction path for identity data, with downstream recomputation after correction or deletion.
When an upstream API returns demographic labels, quarantine or discard them by default rather than importing them into long-term memory.
core_finding: The services encoded gender through restrictive classification schemas, performed worse for transgender people, and could not represent nonbinary identities when their output vocabulary allowed only binary labels; user expectations also conflicted with what the systems encoded.
limitations: This record is based on the publisher abstract and bibliographic metadata. The image sample was self-selected through Instagram labels, the commercial services are 2019 snapshots, and visual presentation is not equivalent to identity; the study does not justify automated gender inference.
method: The paper systematically analyzes ten commercial facial-analysis and image-labeling services, then evaluates five services on a custom dataset built from self-labeled Instagram images representing diverse genders.
population_or_data: A custom collection of self-labeled Instagram images representing diverse genders, ten commercial services in the system analysis, and five services in the empirical evaluation.

architecture_implications: Represent owner-confirmed values as a structured motivational map with compatibility and conflict relations, rather than as independent flat labels.
Treat the map as an explicit self-report layer with versioning and confidence; conversational evidence may suggest an interview question but should not silently assign a value priority.
core_finding: The analyses supported distinguishing 19 values and largely recovered their predicted circular motivational order; the narrower values also produced more specific associations with external beliefs than the original broad value categories.
limitations: The evidence is based mainly on cross-sectional self-report instruments and belief correlates. Cross-sample structural support does not establish that a value score predicts a particular individual's behavior, remains stable over time, or transfers unchanged into private messaging contexts.
method: The authors tested a 19-value instrument with confirmatory factor analysis, multidimensional scaling, and predictive-validity analyses across 15 samples from 10 countries.
population_or_data: 6,059 participants in 15 cross-national samples from 10 countries completed the refined values instrument and measures used for external validation.

limitations: This is self-report scale development, not evidence that the five styles causally determine real decisions or remain invariant across domains. Sample details are absent from the abstract, and some reported internal consistencies are modest.
method: A multistage four-sample instrument-development study used principal-axis factor analysis, item analysis, internal-consistency testing, independence tests, and concurrent-validity analyses.

architecture_implications: Benchmark multiple sparse and dense retrievers because granularity interacts strongly with retrieval model choice.
Construct memory units around coherent topic segments rather than fixed turns or entire sessions.

method: Compared turn-, session-, and summary-level memory; proposed SeCom to segment conversations into topical units and compress them before BM25 or dense retrieval; evaluated on LoCoMo, a reconstructed Long-MT-Bench+, and dialogue-segmentation datasets with ablations and human evaluation.

Represent confidence and memory type separately instead of treating every retrieved artifact as equivalent evidence of recollection.

limitations: The study evaluates image-cued memory under a bounded protocol rather than a complete lifetime archive; image presence is not proof that an interpretation is correct, and the abstract-level evidence does not establish transfer to text-only logs or long-term autonomous agents.

limitations: The reported evaluation is a single-patient clinical trial, so efficacy cannot be generalized to healthy users or other impairments; the abstract does not establish long-term independence from repeated review, and wearable capture creates substantial bystander and intimacy risks.

architecture_implications: Interpret temporary silence about another goal as possible goal shielding, not evidence that the goal has disappeared; confirm status through later observations or owner elicitation.
Maintain an explicit active-goal state with hierarchical, competing, and facilitative edges so retrieval can prioritize a focal goal without deleting temporarily suppressed alternatives.
core_finding: Committed focal goals reduced accessibility of alternatives, especially alternatives serving the same higher-order purpose; shielding weakened when alternatives facilitated the focal goal and was associated with better focal-goal pursuit.
limitations: The studies focus on short-horizon laboratory accessibility and pursuit, often in student samples. Suppression in a task does not establish a durable personal goal hierarchy, and reported psychological moderators must not be repurposed for clinical profiling.
method: Six studies manipulated or measured focal-goal activation and commitment, assessed accessibility of alternative goals, and examined downstream pursuit and attainment.
population_or_data: Human participants, including student samples, completed six laboratory studies of goal accessibility and pursuit; exact sample sizes are not reported in the primary abstract.

architecture_implications: Minimize output detail and rate-limit probing, but do not treat either measure as a substitute for privacy-preserving training and representative evaluation.
Run membership-inference audits against every released personalized model, adapter, embedding index, and confidence-bearing API before deployment.
Use a per-person training manifest so an owner can determine which artifacts may expose whether a person or conversation contributed.
core_finding: Black-box outputs leaked training membership across multiple tasks and services. Overfitting and class representativeness affected attack success, but neither alone explained vulnerability, and simply returning fewer output probabilities did not consistently remove leakage.
limitations: The experiments concern 2017-era classifiers and prediction APIs, not modern generative models. The attack assumes a candidate record and suitable reference data, and measured success varies substantially with task, model, and output interface.
method: The authors train shadow models that mimic a target model and use their member versus non-member outputs to train attack classifiers, testing local and commercial prediction services and several mitigations.
population_or_data: Classification models and APIs evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, MNIST, UCI Adult, retail purchase records, Foursquare location data, and a Texas hospital discharge dataset.

architecture_implications: Keep generated rationales separate from observed actions because synthetic reasoning is not evidence of a user's actual cognition.
Model iterative correction and non-purchase as normal behavior, not noise around a purchase objective.
Train and evaluate on ordered real actions and outcomes rather than plausible narrated intentions.
core_finding: Prompted models achieved at most 11.86% exact next-action accuracy; a fine-tuned model reached 17.26% accuracy and 33.86% F1, while prompted agents over-purchased, overused filters, and failed to reproduce iterative search and revision.
limitations: The domain is online shopping with a simplified action space; textualized HTML omits visual and interaction cues, exact next-action scoring is strict, synthetic reasoning traces are not human explanations, and results do not generalize directly to personal life behavior.
method: Introduced ShopCART from consented beta-shopping logs, compared prompted proprietary and open models, and fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B with synthetic reasoning traces for next-action prediction.

limitations: The study uses retrospective recall and associations between reported appraisals and reported emotions, so it cannot establish causal direction. Sample details are unavailable in the primary abstract, and the recovered dimensions may depend on the emotion list and cultural vocabulary.
method: Participants recalled experiences associated with 15 emotions and rated each experience on proposed appraisal dimensions; dimensional analyses recovered the structure relating appraisals to emotion categories.

architecture_implications: Condition simulations on explicit community rules and diverse personas, and expose those assumptions with every generated trace.
Pair synthetic prototypes with participatory evaluation by the people represented before deployment decisions.

core_finding: Generated communities were plausible enough that evaluators misclassified generated versus real content at an average rate of 41%, and designers found populated prototypes useful for exploring possible behavior, while the authors explicitly reject treating them as point predictions of a real community.
limitations: The technical evaluation is limited to English Reddit communities and plausibility can conceal model bias; a 16-person designer study does not establish downstream validity, generated users can be misused as fake participation, and simulation cannot replace engagement with real communities.
method: Built a system that generates community members and interactions from goals, rules, and seed personas; evaluated outputs against 50 post-GPT-3 subreddits and conducted a design study with 16 social-computing designers.

architecture_implications: Require human calibration for each social dimension because an automated judge's validity is not uniform across constructs.
Score goal completion separately from believability, rapport, norm compliance, and knowledge of the other person.
Test a digital brain in interactive, relationship-conditioned scenarios rather than only scoring isolated replies.
core_finding: Strong language models remained below humans on difficult social interactions, especially where social commonsense and strategic communication mattered. The GPT-4 evaluator tracked human goal ratings better than several social dimensions, where agreement was materially weaker.
limitations: The benchmark uses short, dyadic, fixed-turn episodes, five relationship categories, and no persistent identity across sessions. Automated evaluation can reproduce stereotypes or evaluator bias, and scenario success is not proof of a specific person's behavior.
method: SOTOPIA paired human or language-model agents in generated dyadic social scenarios, scored interactions on seven social dimensions, and compared an LLM evaluator with human judgments on a harder subset.
population_or_data: Dyadic text role-plays spanning five relationship types and varied social goals, with human-agent and agent-agent interactions plus human annotations for evaluator validation.

architecture_implications: Distinguish owner-authored chapter structure from culturally normative milestone labels so the system can use cultural expectations as tentative prompts rather than inferred biography.

architecture_implications: Add adversarial tests that ask about future events, superseded beliefs, absent participants, and facts the owner never supplied.
Create counterfactual probes that distinguish known-at-time, not-yet-known, and never-known information.
Give every autobiographical claim and event a validity interval and filter retrieval to information available at the requested time.
Teach the system to express unawareness when evidence falls outside the active temporal boundary instead of using the base model's world knowledge.
Track temporal leakage as its own failure class even when the generated statement is globally true.
Version owner memory and persona state by observation time, and evaluate responses against the information available at the requested cutoff rather than against an all-time profile.
core_finding: State-of-the-art models, including strong proprietary systems, frequently used future or otherwise unavailable information when answering as a past version of a character. Narrative-Experts reduced these point-in-time hallucinations but did not eliminate them.
limitations: The source material is four English book series rather than real longitudinal lives. Automated judging adds model bias and cost, the questions are structurally constrained, and the proposed mitigation adds latency while remaining incomplete.
method: TimeChara generated temporal character questions from long-form fiction, labeled answers by whether information was available at the target point, evaluated current models primarily with GPT-4 judgments, validated that judge against human ratings on a 50-instance subset, and tested a Narrative-Experts prompting method.
population_or_data: 10,895 instances drawn from four English novel series; the main model comparison used 600 sampled instances, while evaluator validation used 50 of those instances and 27 qualified human annotators.

architecture_implications: Include adversarial tasks where pretrained knowledge should differ from the target person's knowledge.
Include adversarially novel variants and contamination checks when evaluating famous tasks that may appear in pretraining data.
Measure within-person coherence separately from across-population diversity so a single language-model prior is not mistaken for many individuals.
Require fidelity tests for both means and distributions; matching an average while collapsing variance is a failure for a digital brain.
Track whether an answer is supported by personal evidence or by the base model's general knowledge.
Use multiple models and prompt variants to estimate simulator sensitivity rather than treating one generation as a person.
core_finding: Models reproduced major patterns in the first three experiments, but failed as human simulators in the Wisdom of Crowds task because they supplied implausibly accurate obscure facts, demonstrating that apparent behavioral replication can coexist with model-specific nonhuman capabilities.
limitations: Only four relatively simple studies were tested; demographic conditioning was shallow, results are prompt- and model-dependent, training contamination cannot be ruled out, and successful aggregate replication does not establish individual fidelity.
method: Ran Turing Experiments with several GPT model variants on the Ultimatum Game, Garden Path sentences, the Milgram shock experiment, and Wisdom of Crowds, comparing simulated responses with published human findings.
population_or_data: Prompted model participants and published human results from four established behavioral experiments.

limitations: The experiments cover explicit movie ratings and known recurring users and items, not open-ended personal evidence. Learned recurrent states are opaque and predictive; the paper provides no owner correction, causal interpretation, or audit mechanism.

architecture_implications: Fuse actions, expected outcomes, observed outcomes, and affective reactions in one probabilistic model while preserving multiple belief-desire explanations and their likelihoods.
When action and affect cues conflict or only one reaction is observed, surface motive uncertainty and trigger owner clarification instead of forcing a coherent motive after the fact.
core_finding: Observers generally recovered desires, but belief recovery depended on having sufficient and mutually consistent action and reaction evidence; when an action was improbable under the target mental states, observers often failed to recover the belief. The Bayesian model tracked judgments at correlations of at least .95.
limitations: The experiments restrict beliefs and desires to a very small hypothesis space and use staged expressions under shared task knowledge. Real people mask and mix emotions, cultural mappings vary, and strong fit to observer judgments does not validate the inferred hidden state as ground truth.
method: Four experiments and calibration studies manipulated whether an agent wanted, expected, and caused an outcome, varied the emotional evidence shown, and compared human mental-state judgments with Bayesian model predictions.
population_or_data: Adult participants judged staged scenarios and facial reactions across four main experiments, with separate participant samples calibrating reaction likelihoods; stimuli included photographs and short acted movies.

architecture_implications: Log which temporal layer contributed to a recommendation so a short-lived context cannot silently become a durable preference.
Maintain a durable owner model and a separately expiring session or episode model, with an explicit fusion rule at retrieval time.
core_finding: The fused long- and short-term model reportedly improved recommendation quality by 15% to 34% over the compared methods across the two datasets.
limitations: This record is based on abstract inspection, so implementation details, exact sample sizes, and per-baseline results were not independently inspected. Citation and bookmarking events are not private conversational evidence, and predictive gains do not show that the latent states correspond to owner-recognized preferences.
method: The paper constructs session-based temporal graphs and an Injected Preference Fusion procedure that blends long- and short-term graph signals.
population_or_data: A citation network and a social-bookmarking dataset reported by the paper; the accessible abstract does not expose sample sizes.

architecture_implications: Before committing a profile update, compare old and new representations at self, neighborhood, and global levels rather than checking only current-task accuracy.
Preservation losses should be component-wise and observable because an ablation showed that a local-structure term can hurt in at least one model-data pairing.
core_finding: GraphSAIL usually achieved the best average Recall@20 while training much faster than batch retraining; on industrial logs it reduced log loss by 5.84% relative to batch training at about 3.5% of the training time.
limitations: Preserving graph structure can also preserve stale or unwanted behavior, and the method has no owner-level retention or deletion policy. The industrial dataset is only briefly described, updates occur in fixed blocks, and most evidence is offline recommendation quality rather than semantic profile fidelity.
method: GraphSAIL distills each prior graph model's self, local-neighborhood, and global-cluster structure into the next incrementally trained model.
population_or_data: Timestamp-split Gowalla and LastFM user-item graphs tested with two GNN recommenders, plus 22 consecutive days of industrial mobile app-store interaction logs.

architecture_implications: Do not route style from audience role alone: include domain norms, institutional expectations, cultural context, and the personal significance of each audience member.
Represent audience effects as learned, revisable weights rather than a universal hierarchy, and preserve counterexamples where the same audience co-occurs with different styles.
core_finding: All three children varied style, but speech changed even when audience factors were stable; broader social and educational demands and the personal significance of particular people complicated a fixed addressee-to-eavesdropper hierarchy.
limitations: The longitudinal evidence is rich but comes from only three preschool children in one specific sociolinguistic setting. Developmental stage and local Creole-English norms sharply limit generalization to adults or text messaging.
method: Longitudinal analysis of Trinidad Creole and Standard English verb-form variation across changing social situations.
population_or_data: Three preschool children in Trinidad observed longitudinally.
